**Q. A(b) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *Theory & Standards*

**(i)** List the **eight types of lines** used in engineering drawing as per BIS SP 46 / ISO 128, and state the line type and thickness for each. [3 marks]
**(ii)** Explain the procedure for **dimensioning a circular feature** (radius and diameter) with neat sketches. [2 marks]
**(iii)** Differentiate between the **Trim** and **Extend** commands with one example each. [2 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Eight Line Types (BIS SP 46 / ISO 128) [3 marks]:**

| # | Line Type | Description | Line Type | Thickness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Type A** | Visible outline (thick) | Continuous | 0.5 ‚Äì 0.7 mm |
| 2 | **Type B** | Hidden line (thin) | Dashed | 0.25 ‚Äì 0.35 mm |
| 3 | **Type C** | Centerline (thin) | Long-dash dot | 0.25 mm |
| 4 | **Type D** | Construction line (thin) | Chain thin | 0.18 mm |
| 5 | **Type E** | Dimension line (thin) | Continuous | 0.25 mm |
| 6 | **Type F** | Extension line (thin) | Continuous | 0.25 mm |
| 7 | **Type G** | Section hatching (thin) | Continuous | 0.25 mm |
| 8 | **Type H** | Cutting plane line (thick) | Long-dash double-dot | 0.5 mm |

**(ii) Dimensioning Circles [2 marks]:**
- **Diameter dimension (`DIMDIAMETER`):** Used for full circles. The dimension value is preceded by the **√ò symbol**. If the circle is large enough, the dimension is placed **inside** with the text crossing the center. If the circle is too small, the dimension is placed **outside** with a **leader line** pointing to the circle.
- **Radius dimension (`DIMRADIUS`):** Used for arcs (less than a full circle). The dimension value is preceded by the letter **R**. For small radii, an **oblique leader** with an arrow touching the arc is used, and the text is placed outside.

**Example (textual sketch representation):**
```
   ___________
  /     √ò40   \           ‚Üê Diameter inside
 |       ‚Ä¢     |
  \___________/           ‚Üê Radius with leader
       \ 
        \R12              ‚Üê Leader pointing to arc
```

**(iii) Trim vs. Extend [2 marks]:**

| Aspect | **TRIM** | **EXTEND** |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Removes the portion of an object that extends **beyond** a cutting edge (boundary). | Lengthens an object **to meet** a boundary. |
| **Direction** | Shortens the object. | Lengthens the object. |
| **Example** | Two crossing lines form an X; TRIM removes the unwanted part of one line to leave a clean T-junction. | A line falls 2 mm short of a vertical boundary; EXTEND pushes its endpoint to meet the boundary. |
| **Command** | `TRIM` ‚Üí select cutting edges ‚Üí Enter ‚Üí click portion to remove. | `EXTEND` ‚Üí select boundary ‚Üí Enter ‚Üí click object to extend. |

---

### ‚ú¶ OPTION B ‚Äî Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Model Paper, PCMRL507]`

**Q. B(a) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *Geometric Construction*

**Draw the orthographic top view, front view, and side view (with section) of the following 3D object using AutoCAD:**

A rectangular block of dimensions **100 mm (L) √ó 60 mm (W) √ó 50 mm (H)** has a **cylindrical through hole of √ò20 mm** centered on the top face, and a **rectangular slot of 30 mm √ó 20 mm √ó 10 mm deep** cut on the front face (centered).

**List all CAD commands used, layer management, and dimensioning steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Initial Setup [0.5 mark]**
- Template: `A3_metric.dwt`. `UNITS` = mm, decimal. `LIMITS` = `(0,0)` to `(420,297)`. `LTSCALE` = 1.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Layer Setup [0.5 mark]**
- `Outline` (white, continuous, 0.5 mm); `Hidden` (blue, dashed, 0.25 mm); `Center` (red, center, 0.25 mm); `Dimension` (green, 0.25 mm); `Hatch` (grey, 0.25 mm); `Text` (magenta, 0.25 mm); `Construction` (yellow, 0.18 mm).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Layout the Three Views [1.5 marks]**
On `Construction` layer, lay out the three views using **first-angle projection** (BIS standard):
- **Top View:** Upper-left area, $100 \times 60$ rectangle.
- **Front View:** Below the top view, $100 \times 50$ rectangle.
- **Side View (Right):** To the right of the front view, $60 \times 50$ rectangle.
- Use `XLINE` (construction line) to project the **common edges** from top view down to front view and across to side view.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final Geometry on Outline Layer [1.5 marks]**
- **Top View:** Use `RECTANGLE` ‚Üí first corner `(-50, 30)`, opposite `(+50, -30)` (representing 100 √ó 60). Use `CIRCLE` ‚Üí center `(0, 0)`, radius `10` (√ò20 hole).
- **Front View:** Use `RECTANGLE` ‚Üí `(-50, 25)` to `(+50, -25)` (100 √ó 50). Add the slot using `RECTANGLE` ‚Üí `(-15, 15)` to `(+15, 5)` (30 √ó 10, with the top of the slot at 15 mm from the top, depth 10 mm).
- **Side View:** Use `RECTANGLE` ‚Üí `(-30, 25)` to `(+30, -25)` (60 √ó 50). Draw the hole as hidden lines (dashed) at $y = 0$ (center), spanning 60 mm width, and as visible vertical lines (the bore's silhouette) at $x = \pm 10$.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Hidden Lines [0.5 mark]**
- On `Hidden` layer, draw the **invisible edges of the hole** in the front view (two horizontal dashed lines at the top and bottom of the bore projected from the top view). The slot's bottom face is also a hidden line in the side view.

**Step 6 ‚Äî Centerlines [0.5 mark]**
- On `Center` layer, draw the **centerlines of the hole** in all three views (center type). Draw axes of symmetry for each view.

**Step 7 ‚Äî Section View (Side View converted to section) [1 mark]**
- Draw the **cutting plane line** (Type H, long-dash double-dot) passing through the hole axis on the front view.
- Project the **sectional side view** to the right of the front view.
- Use `HATCH` ‚Üí pattern `ANSI31` ‚Üí scale 1.0 ‚Üí pick internal points of the cut material.
- The √ò20 hole now appears as a **visible circle** (not hidden) in the section.

**Step 8 ‚Äî Dimensioning [1 mark]**
- Overall dimensions: 100, 60, 50 mm on the three views.
- Feature dimensions: √ò20 for the hole; 30 √ó 20 √ó 10 for the slot (use 2D dimension for the slot's visible portion: 30 mm length on front view, 10 mm depth via a vertical dimension).
- Section labels (A-A) and cutting-plane identification.

---

**Q. B(b) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *Commands & Modifications*

**(i)** Explain the **OSNAP modes** in detail. List any **8 modes** with their functions. [3 marks]
**(ii)** What is the difference between the **Scale** and **Stretch** commands? Give a suitable example for each. [2 marks]
**(iii)** A line segment is drawn from point `A(10, 20)` to point `B(70, 20)` on the current XY plane. **Calculate the new coordinates** of the point `B` after the following sequence of commands is executed: [2 marks]
   `ROTATE` (about A) by `90¬∞` counter-clockwise ‚Üí `COPY` (from A) at displacement `(@0, 20)` ‚Üí `SCALE` (about A) by factor `2`.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Eight OSNAP Modes [3 marks]:**
1. **ENDpoint (‚ñ¢):** Snaps to the closest endpoint of a line, arc, or polyline segment.
2. **MIDpoint (‚ñ≠):** Snaps to the midpoint of a line or arc segment.
3. **CENter (‚äï):** Snaps to the center of a circle, arc, or ellipse.
4. **INTersection (‚úï):** Snaps to the intersection of two entities.
5. **PERpendicular (‚ä•):** Snaps to the point on an entity that forms a 90¬∞ angle with another entity.
6. **TANgent (‚äô):** Snaps to the point on a circle or arc where a line would be tangent.
7. **QUAdrant (‚óá):** Snaps to the 0¬∞, 90¬∞, 180¬∞, or 270¬∞ point of a circle or arc.
8. **NEArest (‚åñ):** Snaps to the point on an entity closest to the cursor (avoid for precision).
9. **INSertion (‚öì):** Snaps to the insertion point of a block or text.
10. **EXTension (‚§¢):** Snaps along the imaginary extension of a line or arc.

**(ii) Scale vs. Stretch [2 marks]:**

| Aspect | **SCALE** | **STRETCH** |
|---|---|---|
| **Effect** | Resizes the entire selected object **uniformly** about a base point. | Resizes only the portion of the object **inside the crossing window**; vertices outside are unchanged. |
| **Geometric Effect** | Object size changes proportionally. | Object may become distorted (non-uniform scaling). |
| **Example** | A circle of radius 10 scaled by factor 2 becomes a circle of radius 20. | A rectangle with one corner inside a crossing window: the corner moves, but the opposite corner stays put, making a non-rectangular quadrilateral. |
| **Command Syntax** | `SCALE` ‚Üí select ‚Üí base point ‚Üí scale factor. | `STRETCH` ‚Üí crossing-window select ‚Üí base point ‚Üí displacement. |

**(iii) Coordinate Calculation [2 marks]:**

**Given:** $A(10, 20)$ and $B(70, 20)$.

**Step 1: ROTATE by 90¬∞ CCW about A.**
The vector $\vec{AB} = (70 - 10, 20 - 20) = (60, 0)$.
Rotating $(60, 0)$ by $90¬∞$ CCW:
$$(x', y') = (x \cos 90¬∞ - y \sin 90¬∞, \ x \sin 90¬∞ + y \cos 90¬∞)$$
$$(x', y') = (60 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 1, \ 60 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0) = (0, 60)$$

New $B_1 = A + (0, 60) = (10, 20) + (0, 60) = (10, 80)$.

**Step 2: COPY from A with displacement `(@0, 20)`.**
This creates a **new copy** of the line; the original $A$‚Äì$B_1$ remains, and a duplicate $A'$‚Äì$B_2$ is created where $A' = A + (0, 20)$ and $B_2 = B_1 + (0, 20)$.

For the original (which we are tracking):
- $A$ becomes $A + (0, 20) = (10, 40)$.
- $B_1$ becomes $B_1 + (0, 20) = (10, 100)$.

**Step 3: SCALE about A_new by factor 2.**
Now $A_{final} = (10, 40)$ is the base. The vector $\vec{A_{final} B_{final}} = 2 \times (0, 60) = (0, 120)$.

$$B_{final} = A_{final} + (0, 120) = (10, 40) + (0, 120) = (10, 160)$$

**Final Answer:** $B_{final} = (10, 160)$ mm. The point $A$ has moved from $(10, 20)$ to $(10, 40)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in 2D drawing questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing Layer Discipline (‚Äì2 to ‚Äì3 marks):** Drawing visible and hidden lines on the same layer, or not using a separate `Construction` layer. Always freeze the `Construction` layer before final output.
> 2. **Wrong Line Type (‚Äì1 to ‚Äì2 marks):** Using continuous lines for hidden features or centerlines. Hidden lines MUST be dashed; centerlines MUST have long-dash short-dash pattern with center-marks.
> 3. **Inconsistent Dimensions (‚Äì2 to ‚Äì3 marks):** Adding too many dimensions (over-dimensioning) or missing critical ones. **One feature, one dimension** is the rule.
> 4. **Ignoring OSNAP (‚Äì1 mark):** Freehand-approximating endpoints instead of snapping. Examiners check by zooming ‚Äî if endpoints are not pixel-perfect on intersections, marks are cut.
> 5. **Forgetting Title Block / Scale (‚Äì1 mark):** Every drawing must have a complete title block with name, scale, date, and sheet number.
> 6. **Wrong Projection Method (‚Äì3 marks):** Mixing 1st-angle and 3rd-angle projection. **India follows 1st-angle projection** (per BIS).
> 7. **No Section Labeling (‚Äì1 mark):** Section views must show the cutting-plane line, the section direction arrows, and the **A-A** label.
> 8. **Incorrect Hatching (‚Äì1 mark):** Hatching the wrong side of a section, or using the wrong scale (too dense or too sparse).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultra-condensed revision notes ‚Äî read this the night before the exam.**

- üîπ **2D CAD is vector-based**, not pixel-based; geometry is stored as math, ensuring infinite zoom precision.
- üîπ **First-angle projection** is the BIS standard in India; views are placed: **Top View below Front View, Right Side View to the right of Front View**.
- üîπ **Sheet sizes** (ISO A-series): A0 (841√ó1189), A1 (594√ó841), A2 (420√ó594), **A3 (297√ó420)** ‚Üê most common lab size, A4 (210√ó297).
- üîπ **Line weights:** Outline = 0.5‚Äì0.7 mm; Hidden, Center, Dimension, Hatch, Text = 0.25 mm; Construction = 0.18 mm.
- üîπ **Coordinate input methods:** Absolute (`X,Y`), Relative (`@dx,dy`), Polar (`@dist<angle`), Direct Distance Entry.
- üîπ **Essential Draw commands:** LINE, PLINE, CIRCLE, ARC, RECTANGLE, POLYGON, ELLIPSE, SPLINE, POINT, XLINE.
- üîπ **Essential Modify commands:** ERASE, COPY, MOVE, ROTATE, MIRROR, OFFSET, TRIM, EXTEND, FILLET, CHAMFER, SCALE, STRETCH, ARRAY, BREAK, EXPLODE.
- üîπ **Must-know OSNAPs:** ENDpoint, MIDpoint, CENter, INTersection, PERpendicular, TANgent, QUAdrant, INSertion.
- üîπ **Dimensions:** Always `DIMSTYLE` first; one feature = one dimension; never double-dimension; prefer √ò for full circles, R for arcs.
- üîπ **Hatching:** Use `BHATCH`, pick internal point; patterns: `ANSI31` (general), `ANSI32` (cast iron), `ANSI37` (rubber).
- üîπ **Layers:** Always create standard layers before drawing; freeze Construction before plotting.
- üîπ **Mirror tip:** Default `MIRROR` keeps the source; type `Y` to delete source if you only want the mirrored copy.
- üîπ **Fillet/Chamfer radius set first:** Type `R` (or `D` for chamfer) **before** selecting objects, otherwise the command uses the previous value.
- üîπ **Polar Array tip:** The total item count includes the original; angle to fill is the angular sweep, not the angle between items.
- üîπ **Plot scale rule:** Drawing scale √ó viewport scale = 1 (so a 1:2 drawing at 0.5 viewport scale prints at full size on paper).
- üîπ **Keyboard Shortcuts to memorize:** F3 (OSNAP), F7 (Grid), **F8 (Ortho)**, F9 (Snap), F10 (Polar), F11 (O-track).
- üîπ **Viva mantra:** *"Orthogonal projection in 1st angle, dimensioning per ISO 129, layering per BIS SP 46, and plotting with controlled line weights."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creation of 3D Objects from 2D Drawings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL507 - CAD LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creation of 3D Objects from 2D Drawings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# üìò Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Creation of 3D Objects from 2D Drawings** is a fundamental Computer-Aided Design (CAD) modeling operation in which a closed two-dimensional (2D) profile or sketch, drawn on a planar workplane, is transformed into a fully defined three-dimensional (3D) solid or surface model by adding the third dimension (depth, thickness, or rotational sweep) using specialized CAD commands.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCMRL507 (CAD Lab)** syllabus, this experiment falls under the domain of **Solid Modeling using Parametric Feature-Based CAD software** (typically AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, or NX). The 3D objects are generated by applying **base features** such as extrusion, revolution, sweep, and loft to closed 2D regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> A 2D drawing in CAD is essentially a collection of geometric entities (lines, arcs, polylines, splines) lying on a flat XY plane. The "third dimension" (Z-axis) is *synthetically generated* by CAD algorithms (translation, rotation, sweeping) acting upon the closed 2D boundary. The resulting object is a **Boundary Representation (B-Rep)** solid stored using mathematical definitions of vertices, edges, and faces.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **baker making cookies**:

- The **2D drawing** is the **cookie cutter's outline** (a flat steel shape with a closed boundary ‚Äî like a star or a gingerbread man).
- The **3D object** is the **actual cookie** that emerges when you press that cutter into dough and pull it out.

The cookie cutter is *flat* (2D), but the cookie has *thickness* (3D). Similarly:

| Cookie Analogy | CAD Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Flat cookie cutter | Closed 2D polyline/sketch |
| Pushing cutter into dough | EXTRUDE command (linear push along Z) |
| Rolling pin rolling over dough | REVOLVE command (rotating around an axis) |
| Stamping two dough pieces together | BOOLEAN operations (Union, Subtract, Intersect) |
| Final shaped cookie | Solid 3D model in CAD database |

Another simple analogy: **Origami folding**. You start with a flat sheet of paper (2D) and through a series of folds, creases, and operations, you transform it into a crane (3D). CAD modeling from 2D is conceptually similar ‚Äî you start with a flat sketch and apply geometric operations to add depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** Almost every mechanical component ‚Äî a gear, a flange, a bracket, a pressure vessel ‚Äî begins its digital life as a 2D profile. Mastery of 2D-to-3D conversion is therefore the **gateway skill** to finite element analysis (FEA), CNC machining simulation, rapid prototyping (3D printing), and digital manufacturing.

---

## 1.3 Key Standardized Terms (KTU Expected Vocabulary)

- **Profile / Sketch**: A closed 2D region bounded by continuous entities. **MUST be closed** for solid creation.
- **Workplane**: The imaginary flat plane (XY, YZ, or XZ) on which the 2D sketch resides.
- **Base Feature**: The first 3D feature created from a 2D sketch ‚Äî it forms the foundation of the model.
- **Boss**: Material added to a part.
- **Cut**: Material removed from a part.
- **B-Rep (Boundary Representation)**: A solid modeling method that describes a solid by its boundary surfaces (faces), edges, and vertices.
- **CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry)**: A modeling method using Boolean combinations of primitive solids (box, cylinder, sphere, etc.).
- **Extrude**: Linear translation of a 2D profile along an axis to add depth.
- **Revolve**: Rotation of a 2D profile about a defined axis to generate a 3D solid.
- **Sweep**: Translation of a 2D profile along a 2D or 3D path.
- **Loft**: Creation of a 3D solid through multiple cross-sectional profiles blended together.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the Golden Rule:** A 2D profile used to create a 3D solid **must be a single, closed, non-self-intersecting region**. If the profile is open, CAD software will refuse the operation or produce a *surface* instead of a *solid*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# üìó Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Methods of 2D-to-3D Conversion

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the following techniques. Each method is a **mathematically defined transformation** applied to a 2D boundary.

### Method 1: **Extrusion** (Most Common ‚Äî Linear Sweep)
- **Concept**: The closed 2D profile is translated (pushed) along a straight vector perpendicular to the sketch plane, typically along the **+Z axis** by a specified distance.
- **Mathematical Operation**:

$$S_{extrude} = \iint_{A} dz \, dA$$

where $A$ is the area of the 2D profile and the profile is swept a distance $h$ along the Z-axis.

- **Resulting Volume**:

$$V_{extrude} = A_{profile} \times h$$

- **Typical Use**: Brackets, plates, blocks, housings, and rectangular prismatic parts.

### Method 2: **Revolution** (Rotational Sweep)
- **Concept**: The 2D profile is rotated about a fixed **axis of revolution** (a line or polyline defined in the sketch) through a specified angle (typically $0¬∞$ to $360¬∞$).
- **Mathematical Operation** (Pappus Theorem):

$$V_{revolve} = 2\pi \times \bar{r} \times A_{profile}$$

where $\bar{r}$ is the distance from the centroid of the profile to the axis of revolution.

- **Resulting Volume** for a full $360¬∞$ revolution:

$$V_{revolve} = 2\pi \cdot d_{centroid} \cdot A_{profile}$$

- **Typical Use**: Shafts, pulleys, bottles, vases, screw threads (helical), flanges.

### Method 3: **Sweep** (Path-Based Sweep)
- **Concept**: The 2D profile is moved along a defined **2D or 3D path** (open or closed) while maintaining its orientation.
- **Mathematical Operation**: Parametric ‚Äî the cross-section $A(s)$ is swept along a curve $\vec{r}(s)$ parameterized by arc length $s$.

$$V_{sweep} = \int_{0}^{L} A(s) \, ds$$

- **Typical Use**: Pipes, springs, wires, cables, structural members with variable cross-section.

### Method 4: **Loft** (Multi-Section Blending)
- **Concept**: Two or more distinct 2D profiles (cross-sections) on different planes are blended together to form a smooth 3D transition.
- **Typical Use**: Aircraft fuselages, boat hulls, transition ducts, ergonomic handles.

---

## 2.2 Boolean Operations (CSG-Based Modification)

After base feature creation, Boolean operations combine, subtract, or intersect solids:

| Boolean Operation | Symbol | Effect | CAD Command |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Union** | $A \cup B$ | Combines two solids into one | UNION / ADD |
| **Subtract** | $A - B$ | Removes solid B from solid A | SUBTRACT / CUT |
| **Intersect** | $A \cap B$ | Keeps only the common volume | INTERSECT |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Boolean operations in CAD follow the mathematical axioms of **set theory**. Union is analogous to OR, Subtract to set difference, and Intersect to AND. The order of operands matters for Subtract.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extrusion Volume | $V = A \times h$ | $A$ = profile area, $h$ = extrusion height | Plate, block, bracket |
| Revolution Volume (Pappus) | $V = 2\pi \bar{r} \cdot A$ | $\bar{r}$ = centroidal distance to axis | Shaft, pulley, bottle |
| Revolution Surface Area | $A_{surf} = 2\pi \bar{r} \cdot L$ | $L$ = profile perimeter length | Bottle exterior |
| Sweep Volume (uniform) | $V = A \times L_{path}$ | $L_{path}$ = path length | Pipe, cable |
| Union Volume | $V_{union} = V_A + V_B - V_{A \cap B}$ | Inclusion-exclusion | Assembled part |
| Subtract Volume | $V_{sub} = V_A - V_{A \cap B}$ | A is body, B is tool | Machined pocket, hole |
| Intersect Volume | $V_{int} = V_{A \cap B}$ | Common region | Boolean check |
| Surface Area of Extrude | $A_{total} = 2A_{profile} + P \times h$ | $P$ = profile perimeter | Painting, coating estimation |
| Centroid Distance (Pappus) | $\bar{r} = \dfrac{\int r \, dA}{\int dA}$ | First moment of area | Offset axes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** The vertical bar $\vert$ for "absolute value" or "divides" is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of 2D-to-3D** |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Design** | Gears, shafts, brackets, engine blocks |
| **Automotive** | Car body panels, chassis, interior components |
| **Aerospace** | Wing sections, turbine blades, fuselage (lofted) |
| **Architecture** | Building facades, structural columns, staircases |
| **Biomedical** | Custom prosthetics, implants (patient-specific) |
| **Manufacturing** | Tool paths for CNC, injection mold cores |
| **3D Printing** | Direct STL generation for additive manufacturing |
| **FEA / Simulation** | Mesh generation for stress, thermal, fluid analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Skill Outcome:** After completing this experiment, students should be able to demonstrate competency in CO2: *"Create 3D models of mechanical components using CAD software"* ‚Äî this is a direct CO mapping for PCMRL507.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# üìô Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume of an Extruded Solid

**Problem Setup:** A 2D profile with area $A$ lies in the XY plane. It is extruded along the Z-axis by a distance $h$. Derive the volume of the resulting 3D solid.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal element of area $dA$ in the XY plane.

$$dA = dx \, dy$$

**Step 2:** When this element is extruded by height $h$, it sweeps out a differential volume $dV$:

$$dV = dA \times dz = dA \times h$$

**Step 3:** Integrate over the entire closed 2D region $A$:

$$V = \int\int_{A} dV = \int\int_{A} h \, dA$$

**Step 4:** Since $h$ is constant for a uniform extrusion:

$$V = h \cdot \int\int_{A} dA = h \cdot A$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{extrude} = A \times h}$$

This is the **fundamental extrude volume formula** used universally in CAD property calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume of a Revolved Solid (Pappus's Second Theorem)

**Problem Setup:** A 2D profile of area $A$ is revolved about an external axis (in the same plane) through a full $360¬∞$. Derive the volume of the resulting solid.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal element $dA$ at a perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis of revolution.

**Step 2:** When the profile rotates through angle $d\theta$, this element traces an arc of length $r \, d\theta$.

**Step 3:** The differential volume swept by $dA$ is the area of the arc-swept region (annulus sector):

$$dV = r \, d\theta \cdot dA$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the full revolution ($\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$) and the entire area $A$:

$$V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{A} r \, d\theta \, dA$$

**Step 5:** Separate the integrals (since $r$ does not depend on $\theta$):

$$V = \left( \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \right) \left( \int_{A} r \, dA \right)$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the angular integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi$$

**Step 7:** The area integral is the **first moment of area** about the axis, which equals $\bar{r} \cdot A$ (by definition of centroidal distance):

$$\int_{A} r \, dA = \bar{r} \cdot A$$

**Step 8:** Combine:

$$V = 2\pi \cdot \bar{r} \cdot A$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{revolve} = 2\pi \bar{r} A}$$

This is **Pappus's Second Centroid Theorem**, and it elegantly computes volumes of revolution *without integration over the rotation angle* ‚Äî only the profile's area and centroid location are needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Surface Area of an Extruded Solid (for Coating/Paint Estimation)

**Problem Setup:** A closed 2D profile with area $A$ and perimeter $P$ is extruded by height $h$. Find the total surface area.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The resulting solid has three types of faces:
- Two end faces (top and bottom) ‚Äî both are copies of the original 2D profile.
- One lateral face (the "skin") ‚Äî formed by sweeping the perimeter.

**Step 2:** Area of the two end faces:

$$A_{ends} = 2 \times A$$

**Step 3:** Area of the lateral face (perimeter √ó height):

$$A_{lateral} = P \times h$$

**Step 4:** Total surface area:

$$A_{total} = A_{ends} + A_{lateral}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{A_{surface} = 2A + P \cdot h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is used to estimate paint, plating, or anodizing quantities for prismatic parts. For example, a steel plate $200 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ has $A = 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$, $P = 600 \text{ mm}$, $h = 10 \text{ mm}$, giving a total surface of $46{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$ for surface treatment costing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Command-to-Geometry Mapping

| **CAD Command** | **Input Requirement** | **Geometric Transformation** | **Output Type** | **Sketch Closure Required?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| EXTRUDE | Closed 2D profile + height | Translation along vector | Solid or Surface | Yes (for solid) |
| REVOLVE | Closed 2D profile + axis + angle | Rotation about axis | Solid or Surface | Yes (for solid) |
| SWEEP | Closed 2D profile + 2D/3D path | Translation along path | Solid or Surface | Yes (for solid) |
| LOFT | 2 or more 2D profiles | Interpolation between sections | Solid or Surface | Yes (each section) |
| UNION | 2 or more solids | Boolean addition | Single solid | N/A |
| SUBTRACT | Solid A + Solid B | Boolean subtraction | Modified solid | N/A |
| INTERSECT | 2 or more solids | Boolean intersection | Common solid | N/A |
| PRESS PULL | Closed boundary on solid face | Direct depth modification | Modified solid | N/A (existing face) |
| THICKEN | Closed surface | Offset both sides | Hollow solid | Surface must be closed |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Volume Calculation for a Cylindrical Step Shaft

**Problem:** A 2D profile is revolved $360¬∞$ about its central axis to form a solid cylinder of diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ and height $H = 80 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the volume using the extrude formula (since a rectangle revolved gives a cylinder, or equivalently a circle extruded).

### Solution:

**Method 1: Extrude Approach**
- 2D profile = circle of diameter $50 \text{ mm}$, so radius $r = 25 \text{ mm}$.
- Profile area:

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (25)^2 = 625\pi \text{ mm}^2$$

- Extrusion height: $h = 80 \text{ mm}$.
- Volume:

$$V = A \times h = 625\pi \times 80 = 50{,}000\pi \text{ mm}^2$$

$$V \approx 157{,}080 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Method 2: Standard Cylinder Formula (Verification)**
- Standard formula: $V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h$

$$V_{cyl} = \pi (25)^2 (80) = 50{,}000\pi \approx 157{,}080 \text{ mm}^3$$

‚úÖ **Both methods agree**, confirming the validity of the extrude formula $V = A \cdot h$.

> [!TIP]
> This cross-verification is the **standard valuation trick** KTU examiners use to award full marks for derivation problems.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CAD Solid Modeling Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[2D Sketch Geometry]
        A2[Workplane Definition]
        A3[User Parameters: Height, Angle, Path]
    end

        B1[Sketch Validator]

    end
```

**Explanation of Architecture:**

- **Input Layer**: Receives the 2D drawing entities, the workplane (XY/YZ/XZ), and user-specified parameters.
- **Kernel Layer**: This is the heart of the CAD system (e.g., Parasolid by Siemens, ACIS by Spatial). It validates the sketch, performs geometric transformations, executes Boolean operations, and builds the B-Rep (Boundary Representation) data structure.
- **Output Layer**: Renders the 3D model, computes engineering properties, and exports to standard formats for downstream use (CAM, FEA, 3D printing).

# üìí Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## üìù Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the EXTRUDE command as used in CAD solid modeling. List the essential geometric requirements for a 2D profile to be successfully extruded into a 3D solid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The EXTRUDE command is a CAD operation that creates a 3D solid or surface by translating a closed 2D profile along a straight vector (typically perpendicular to the sketch plane) by a user-specified distance (height/depth).

**Essential Geometric Requirements (1.5 marks):**
1. The 2D profile must be a **single, closed region** with no open ends.
2. The profile must be **non-self-intersecting** (no overlapping boundary segments).
3. The profile must lie entirely on a **single planar workplane** (XY, YZ, or XZ).
4. There must be **no duplicate or coincident entities** within the profile.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "profile must be closed" but **fail to mention "non-self-intersecting"**. This is a common 0.5-mark deduction. Always state BOTH conditions.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State Pappus's Second Centroid Theorem and explain its application in calculating the volume of a revolved solid using CAD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Theorem Statement (2 marks):** *Pappus's Second Centroid Theorem* states that the volume of a solid of revolution generated by rotating a planar 2D region (profile) about an external axis in the same plane is equal to the product of the area of the region and the distance traveled by its centroid:

$$V = 2\pi \bar{r} A$$

where $\bar{r}$ is the perpendicular distance from the centroid of the profile to the axis of revolution, and $A$ is the area of the profile.

**Application in CAD (1 mark):** When using the REVOLVE command, the CAD kernel internally applies this theorem to compute the volume of the resulting solid. For example, revolving a rectangle (with centroid offset from the axis) by $360¬∞$ gives a hollow cylinder (torus-like or annular solid), and the volume is calculated instantly as $V = 2\pi \bar{r} A_{rect}$.

---

## üìù Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts are compulsory within a choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the step-by-step procedure to create a 3D solid model of a flange (shown below) from its 2D drawing using the REVOLVE command. (7 marks)**

*2D Profile Description:* A L-shaped profile (height $H = 60 \text{ mm}$, outer radius $R = 80 \text{ mm}$, inner radius $r = 40 \text{ mm}$, thickness $t = 20 \text{ mm}$) to be revolved $360¬∞$ about the central vertical axis.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Open the CAD software and create a new part file. Set the workplane to the **Front Plane (YZ)** or **XY plane** depending on software convention.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Sketch the closed 2D L-shaped profile using **LINE, ARC, and TRIM** commands. Define the dimensions:
- Outer arc: $R = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Inner arc: $r = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Vertical height: $H = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Thickness: $t = 20 \text{ mm}$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Draw the **axis of revolution** as a construction line (centerline) passing through the central vertical axis. In most CAD systems, this is done using the **Centerline** tool in the sketch environment.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Verify that the profile is **closed** (use "Close" or "Check Sketch" tool). Apply all dimensional constraints and relations (concentricity, symmetry, equal).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Exit the sketch environment and activate the **REVOLVE** (or "Revolved Boss/Base") command. Select:
- **Profile**: Click the closed L-shaped region.
- **Axis**: Click the centerline.
- **Angle**: Specify $360¬∞$ (full revolution).

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Click **OK/Apply** to generate the 3D solid. The CAD engine applies Pappus's theorem internally to compute volume:

$$V = 2\pi \bar{r} \cdot A_{profile}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Verify the model using **Mass Properties / Section View / Measure Tool**. Save the file in `.SLDPRT` (SolidWorks), `.PRT` (Creo), or `.CATPart` (CATIA) format, and export as `.STEP` or `.IGES` for interoperability.

---

**(b) Differentiate between EXTRUDE, REVOLVE, SWEEP, and LOFT commands with suitable 2D-to-3D examples and applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Command** | **Definition** | **2D Input** | **3D Output Example** | **Industrial Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EXTRUDE** | Linear translation of profile along a vector | Closed profile + height $h$ | Rectangular block, plate, L-bracket | Brackets, housings, plates |
| **REVOLVE** | Rotation of profile about an axis | Closed profile + axis + angle | Cylinder, cone, torus, pulley | Shafts, flanges, bottles, threads |
| **SWEEP** | Translation of profile along a path | Closed profile + 2D/3D path | Pipe along curve, spring, wire | Pipes, cables, springs, rails |
| **LOFT** | Blending between two or more profiles | 2+ profiles on different planes | Aircraft fuselage, boat hull | Aerodynamic bodies, transition ducts |

**Key Differences (4 marks allocated as 1 mark per differentiating aspect):**

1. **Geometric Transformation Type:**
   - EXTRUDE: Pure linear translation.
   - REVOLVE: Pure rotation.
   - SWEEP: Translation along arbitrary path.
   - LOFT: Interpolation between multiple cross-sections.

2. **Number of Input Sketches:**
   - EXTRUDE: 1 profile.
   - REVOLVE: 1 profile + 1 axis line.
   - SWEEP: 1 profile + 1 path.
   - LOFT: 2 or more profiles.

3. **Mathematical Foundation:**
   - EXTRUDE: $V = A \times h$
   - REVOLVE: $V = 2\pi \bar{r} A$
   - SWEEP: $V = \int A(s) ds$
   - LOFT: Spline-based interpolation (no simple closed-form volume).

4. **Path / Axis Dependency:**
   - EXTRUDE: Requires only distance.
   - REVOLVE: Requires axis line.
   - SWEEP: Requires path curve.
   - LOFT: Requires alignment of multiple cross-sections.

(1 mark for clean tabular presentation and 1 mark for industrial application examples.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) A 2D rectangular profile of dimensions $100 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm}$ is extruded to a height of $30 \text{ mm}$. Calculate: (i) the volume of the resulting 3D solid, and (ii) the total surface area. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Profile: Rectangle, length $L = 100 \text{ mm}$, width $W = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Extrusion height: $h = 30 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 (1 mark): Calculate Profile Area**

$$A_{profile} = L \times W = 100 \times 50 = 5000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Calculate Volume**

Using the extrude volume formula:

$$V = A_{profile} \times h = 5000 \times 30$$

$$\boxed{V = 150{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^3}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Calculate Profile Perimeter**

$$P = 2(L + W) = 2(100 + 50) = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Calculate Total Surface Area**

Using the derived formula:

$$A_{total} = 2A_{profile} + P \times h$$

$$A_{total} = 2(5000) + 300 \times 30 = 10{,}000 + 9{,}000$$

$$\boxed{A_{total} = 19{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 1.9 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): State Units and Verification**
- Volume: $150{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 150 \text{ cm}^3$ ‚úÖ (consistent with rectangular block geometry)
- Surface area: $19{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.019 \text{ m}^2$ ‚úÖ

---

**(b) With the aid of a neat sketch (describe in words), explain the procedure to convert a 2D drawing of a stepped shaft into a 3D solid using EXTRUDE and Boolean SUBTRACT operations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Open the part file and select the **Top Plane (XY)**. Sketch two concentric circles:
- Outer circle: $D_1 = 40 \text{ mm}$ (larger diameter section)
- Inner circle: $D_2 = 25 \text{ mm}$ (smaller diameter section)

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Use the **EXTRUDE** command to extrude the larger circle ($40 \text{ mm}$ diameter) by $h_1 = 80 \text{ mm}$ along the +Z direction. This creates the base cylindrical boss.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Create a new sketch on the **top face** of the first cylinder. Sketch a circle of diameter $D_2 = 25 \text{ mm}$ concentric with the existing boss.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Use the **EXTRUDE** command again to add the smaller cylindrical section (boss) of height $h_2 = 50 \text{ mm}$ on top. This is a **Boolean UNION** operation automatically performed by the CAD system (Add boss to base).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** To create a **central through-hole**, sketch a circle of diameter $D_{hole} = 15 \text{ mm}$ on the top face (concentric with all axes). Extrude this circle *through both cylinders* using the **"Through All"** option, and apply the **Boolean SUBTRACT** operation (Cut) to remove material.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Add **chamfers** (e.g., $1 \text{ mm} \times 45¬∞$) at the ends of the shaft and **fillets** at the step junction using the appropriate CAD tools.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Verify using the **Mass Properties** tool: confirm volume, centroid location, and moment of inertia. Save and export as `.STEP` for manufacturing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to mention concentricity constraints** between the two cylindrical sections. Without concentricity, the BOOLEAN UNION will fail or produce a non-axisymmetric geometry. Always explicitly state that all circles must be **concentric** with a common centerline.

---

# üìò SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part Drawing (Component Drawing):** A part drawing is a **detailed 2D orthographic engineering drawing** of a single machine component that completely defines its **geometry, dimensions, tolerances, surface finish, and material specifications** using standardized conventions laid down by **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) ‚Äî IS 696 / IS 15021 / SP 46**, and aligned with **ISO 128** for general principles of technical drawing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme CAD Lab, **"Creation of Part Drawings"** refers to the process of generating **production-ready 2D orthographic projections of individual machine components** (such as shafts, bearings, pulleys, bushings, etc.) using a CAD package like **AutoCAD / SolidWorks / NX-CAD / CATIA**, strictly following the drawing conventions mandated by **KTU's drafting standards (IS 696:1972 ‚Äì Code of Practice for General Engineering Drawings)**.

A standard part drawing must contain the following elements:

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Views** | Orthographic (front, top, side) and/or sectional views |
| **Centerlines & Hidden Lines** | Symmetry axes and non-visible edges |
| **Dimensions** | Linear, angular, radial, diametral |
| **Tolerances** | Dimensional, geometric (GD&T) |
| **Surface Finish Symbols** | Roughness indication (Ra values) |
| **Title Block & Parts List** | Drawing number, scale, material, projection symbol |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy ‚Äî The "Architect's Blueprint" of a Machine Part:**
> Imagine you order a custom wooden chair from a carpenter. To make sure the carpenter builds the **exact chair** you want, you hand over a **blueprint** with multiple views (top, front, side), exact measurements, and notes on the wood type. A **Part Drawing** is precisely this blueprint, but for a **mechanical engineer and a machinist**. It is the **universal language** that allows any engineer in the world to manufacture or inspect a part accurately, without ambiguity.

In the CAD Lab, instead of drawing on a sheet of paper with a T-square and drafter, students use a **mouse and keyboard** to "construct" this blueprint digitally ‚Äî but every line, dimension, and symbol must follow the same **engineering rules** that govern manual drawing.

---

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Outcomes Mapped (COs for CAD Lab ‚Äî PCMRL507):**
> - **CO2:** Create 2D part drawings of machine components using CAD software, applying standard drafting conventions.
> - **CO3:** Apply sectional views, dimensioning, and tolerance practices as per IS 696 / SP 46.
> - **CO4:** Generate production-ready manufacturing drawings with title blocks and parts lists.
>
> **Bloom's Cognitive Levels Targeted:** *Apply (L3)*, *Analyze (L4)*, *Create (L6)*.

The experiment is **hands-on** ‚Äî students are graded on the **accuracy of the drawing**, the **correct use of drafting standards**, and the **completeness** of the part drawing (all required views, dimensions, and notations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# üìó SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ‚Äî The Logic Behind Part Drawings

A part drawing is not just a picture ‚Äî it is a **legally binding technical contract** between the designer and the manufacturer. The theory behind it is built on three pillars:

### üîπ Pillar 1: Orthographic Projection Theory
- Based on **parallel projection** where projection lines are perpendicular to the projection plane.
- A 3D object is "unfolded" into multiple 2D views following **First Angle Projection (IS convention used in India & Europe)** or **Third Angle Projection (ANSI/US convention)**.
- **KTU Standard:** Always **First Angle Projection** ‚Äî the symbol is a truncated cone viewed from the left.

### üîπ Pillar 2: Dimensional Theory
- Every feature on the part must be **dimensioned exactly once** (avoiding dual or chain redundancy unless necessary).
- Dimensions are placed **between the views they belong to**, not inside them.
- A **dimension has three components**: numerical value, unit (default mm), and tolerance.

### üîπ Pillar 3: Sectional View Theory
- When internal features are hidden behind solid material, a **sectional view** is used ‚Äî the part is conceptually "cut" along a plane, and the cut surface is shown with **sectional hatching (45¬∞ parallel lines, spacing 2‚Äì3 mm)**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for Creating a Part Drawing

The KTU lab exam typically requires following a **structured workflow**. Below is the exact logical sequence students should follow:

1. **Analyze the 3D component (or isometric sketch provided).**
2. **Identify principal views:** Decide which face is the *front view* (usually the most informative face).
3. **Set up the drawing sheet:** Select a standard A4/A3 sheet size; insert the **title block and border** as per IS 15021.
4. **Set up layers** in CAD: `Construction` (centerlines, hidden), `Object` (visible edges), `Dimension`, `Hatch`, `Text`.
5. **Draw the centerlines** (chain thin lines: long‚Äìshort‚Äìlong pattern).
6. **Block out the views** in light construction geometry to position the front, top, and side views correctly.
7. **Project the views** from the front view to the top and side using CAD projection tools.
8. **Solidify object lines** ‚Äî dark, continuous thick lines (typically 0.5 mm width).
9. **Add hidden lines** for non-visible internal features (dashed thin lines).
10. **Apply sectional view (if required):**
    - Draw the cutting plane line (chain thick with arrows).
    - Replace the front view with the sectioned view.
    - Apply hatching at 45¬∞ to cut surfaces.
11. **Dimension the drawing:**
    - Linear dimensions (horizontal, vertical, aligned).
    - Diametral dimensions with √ò symbol.
    - Radial dimensions with R symbol.
    - Angular dimensions.
12. **Add tolerances, surface finish, and notes.**
13. **Insert title block** with part name, scale, drawn by, checked by, date, material, and projection symbol.
14. **Verify and plot/print.**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ‚Äî Drawing Standards Quick Recall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam-grade cheat sheet**. Memorize it for the KTU ESE viva and lab examination.

| Standard Feature | IS Code Reference | KTU Conventional Value / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Line types (visible, hidden, center, section) | IS 696:1972 | 4 types ‚Äî thick continuous, thin continuous, thin dashed, chain thin |
| Object line thickness | IS 696 | **0.5 mm** (thick, continuous) |
| Hidden line thickness | IS 696 | **0.25 mm** (thin, dashed, ~3 mm dashes, 1 mm gaps) |
| Centerline pattern | IS 696 | Long‚Äìshort‚Äìlong (~10 mm long, 2 mm short) |
| Lettering height | IS 9601 | **5 mm** (uppercase) for general notes; 7 mm for titles |
| Projection method (India) | IS 696 | **First Angle Projection** |
| Hatching angle | IS 696 | **45¬∞** to horizontal, equally spaced lines |
| Hatching spacing | IS 696 | **2 to 3 mm** between parallel lines |
| Dimension line spacing | IS 15021 | **10 mm** between parallel dimension lines |
| Arrowhead size | IS 15021 | **3 mm long, 1 mm wide** filled triangle |
| Dimension termination | IS 15021 | **Arrowheads OR oblique strokes** (KTU prefers arrowheads) |
| Title block position | IS 15021 | **Bottom-right corner** of the drawing sheet |
| Sheet sizes | IS 10711 | A0, A1, A2, A3, A4, A5 |
| Surface finish symbol | IS 15269 | Tick (‚úì) on the surface with Ra value |
| Geometric tolerances | IS 14500 / ISO 1101 | GD&T ‚Äî form, orientation, location, runout |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Part Drawings

- üè≠ **Manufacturing:** Acts as the primary input for CNC programming and manual machining.
- üîç **Quality Control (QC):** Inspection teams use it to verify the produced part against design intent.
- üì¶ **Procurement & Vendor Communication:** Universal language for sourcing components globally.
- ‚öñÔ∏è **Legal & IP Protection:** Patent drawings and contractual documents.
- üõÝÔ∏è **Assembly Drawings:** Multiple part drawings combine to form an assembly drawing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# üìô SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since part drawing creation is a **procedural and standards-based** process (not a calculus problem), the "derivations" below are the **exhaustive step-by-step analytical procedures** that demonstrate the logic behind drafting decisions. Each step is **explicitly stated** with no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Procedural Derivation ‚Äî How to Decide the Number and Type of Views

A common KTU question is: *"How many views are required for a given component?"* Let us derive the analytical decision matrix:

### Step 1 ‚Äî Classify the Component
Classify the component by symmetry and feature density.

$$
\text{View Count Decision} = f(\text{Symmetry}, \text{Internal Features}, \text{Complexity})
$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the Rule of Six

$$
\text{Required Views} = 
\begin{cases}
1 \text{ view} & \text{if component is a flat plate of uniform thickness (single orthographic view suffices)} \\
2 \text{ views} & \text{if component is axially symmetric (e.g., full cylinder, cone) ‚Äî front + one side} \\
3 \text{ views} & \text{if component has no symmetry in at least two directions (general case)} \\
3 \text{ views} + \text{section} & \text{if internal features are complex and not clear in hidden lines}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Validate with the Hidden Line Rule

$$
\text{If } N_{\text{hidden lines}} > 4 \text{ in any single view} \implies \text{Add a sectional view}
$$

where $N_{\text{hidden lines}}$ is the count of dashed line segments in the view.

### Step 4 ‚Äî Final View Configuration

$$
\boxed{\text{Output} = \{ \text{Front, Top, Side (or ISO)} \} \cup \{ \text{Section Views} \} \cup \{ \text{Detail Views} \}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Procedural Derivation ‚Äî Dimensioning Logic (Chain vs. Parallel)

This is a frequently tested concept in the KTU ESE viva.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify Feature Categories

$$
\text{Features} = \{ \text{Lengths}, \text{Diameters}, \text{Radii}, \text{Angles}, \text{Holes}, \text{Slots}, \text{Fillets} \}
$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the "Dimension Once" Rule

$$
\forall \text{ feature } f_i : \dim(f_i) \text{ is defined exactly once in the entire drawing}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Choose Between Chain and Parallel Dimensioning

$$
\text{Dimensioning Method} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Chain (Series)} & \text{if tolerance accumulation is acceptable, e.g., hole spacing} \\
\text{Parallel} & \text{if a common datum is critical, e.g., from a reference edge} \\
\text{Coordinated} & \text{if both directions matter, e.g., bolt pattern (preferred KTU choice)} \\
\text{Combined} & \text{if multiple features share a baseline}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Datum Selection Logic

$$
\text{Datum} = \arg\max_{f} \left[ \text{Machinist's Accessibility} \times \text{Functional Importance} \right]
$$

The datum is the reference edge, face, or axis from which most critical dimensions are taken. The chosen datum must be **physically reachable** by a machinist's measuring tool (e.g., caliper jaw, micrometer anvil).

---

## 3.3 Procedural Derivation ‚Äî Sectional View Decision

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify Hidden Internal Features

$$
F_{\text{hidden}} = \{ f : f \in \text{internal} \land f \text{ is critical to function} \}
$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Check Sectioning Necessity

$$
\text{If } \mid F_{\text{hidden}} \mid \geq 2 \land \text{hidden lines obscure clarity} \implies \text{Section Required}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Choose Section Type

$$
\text{Section Type} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Full Section} & \text{if the cutting plane passes completely through the part} \\
\text{Half Section} & \text{if the part is symmetric ‚Äî one half section, one half external view} \\
\text{Offset Section} & \text{if features do not align on a single plane} \\
\text{Removed Section} & \text{if a small cross-sectional area needs clarification, drawn elsewhere} \\
\text{Aligned Section} & \text{if features are rotated to align with the cutting plane}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Hatching Application

$$
\text{Hatching Angle} = 45¬∞ \text{ to horizontal (or to the major axis of the part)}
$$

$$
\text{Hatching Spacing} = 2 \text{ mm to } 3 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Same Part} \implies \text{Same Hatch Direction and Spacing}
$$

$$
\text{Different Parts in Assembly} \implies \text{Different Hatch Directions (} 45¬∞, 135¬∞, 0¬∞, 90¬∞ \text{) and Spacings}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî KTU Lab Evaluation Rubric (Predicted)

| Evaluation Criterion | Weightage | What Examiners Look For |
|---|---|---|
| Correct view selection & projection method | **20%** | First Angle symbol, correct view layout |
| Accuracy of geometric construction | **20%** | Proportions, alignments, parallelism |
| Line type correctness | **10%** | Thick for visible, thin for hidden, chain for center |
| Dimensioning completeness & standard | **20%** | All features dimensioned, units, no redundancy |
| Sectioning (if applicable) | **10%** | Cutting plane line, hatching, correct view replacement |
| Title block & metadata | **10%** | All fields filled, correct format |
| Tolerance & surface finish notation | **10%** | GD&T symbols, Ra values |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example ‚Äî Analytical Walkthrough

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** *"The 3D isometric view of a stepped shaft with a keyway is given. Create the part drawing."*

### Solution Walkthrough:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify symmetry:** The shaft is **axially symmetric** (rotationally symmetric about its main axis).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply view count formula:**

$$
\text{Axially Symmetric} \implies \text{Front view (full length)} + \text{One sectional view (end-on, to show keyway and concentric features)}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{View Configuration} = \{ \text{Front View}, \text{Sectional End View (Right Side)} \}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Dimensioning strategy:**

$$
\text{Dimensions} = \{ L_1, L_2, L_3, \varnothing D_1, \varnothing D_2, \varnothing D_3, w_{\text{keyway}}, d_{\text{keyway}}, R \text{ (fillet radii)} \}
$$

All lengths are **parallel-dimensioned** from the left datum (the end face of the shaft). All diameters use the **√ò symbol**, and the keyway width and depth are **directly dimensioned** on the sectional view.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Tolerance assignment:**

$$
\varnothing D_1 = \varnothing 50 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm (medium tolerance ‚Äî running fit)}
$$

$$
\varnothing D_2 = \varnothing 30^{+0.000}_{-0.013} \text{ mm (H7 ‚Äî press fit with bearing)}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Surface finish:**

$$
\text{Keyway sides} : Ra = 1.6 \ \mu m \quad ; \quad \text{Outer cylindrical surfaces} : Ra = 0.8 \ \mu m
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Title block population:**

$$
\{ \text{Part Name: SHAFT, DWG No: SHF-001, Scale: 1:2, Material: EN8, Projection: First Angle} \}
$$

# üìí SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 üìù Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (2 √ó 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define a part drawing. List any four essential elements that must be present in a part drawing as per IS 696.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **part drawing** is a detailed 2D orthographic engineering drawing of an individual machine component that conveys its complete geometry, dimensions, tolerances, surface finish, and material using standardized drafting conventions (IS 696:1972).

**Four essential elements:**
1. **Orthographic views** (front, top, side as required) showing true shape and size.
2. **Dimensions** (linear, angular, diametral, radial) with units and tolerances.
3. **Title block** containing drawing number, scale, material, name, and projection symbol.
4. **Line conventions** (visible, hidden, center, section) per IS 696 with prescribed thickness.

*(1 mark for definition + ¬Ω mark √ó 4 for elements = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. What is the difference between First Angle and Third Angle Projection? Which one is used in KTU examinations?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | First Angle Projection | Third Angle Projection |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | **IS / ISO (Europe, India)** | **ANSI (USA, Canada)** |
| Symbol | Truncated cone with small diameter on right | Truncated cone with small diameter on left |
| Top view position | **Below** front view | **Above** front view |
| Left side view position | **Right** of front view | **Left** of front view |

> **KTU Standard:** **First Angle Projection** is mandatory. The projection symbol (truncated cone) must be drawn in the title block.

*(2 marks for comparison + 1 mark for KTU convention = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 üìù Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 √ó 14 = 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Given the isometric view of a machine component [e.g., a stepped bushing with internal bore and a key slot], draw the part drawing using a CAD package. The drawing must include:**
**(a) All required orthographic and sectional views with correct projection method. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Complete dimensioning, tolerancing, surface finish, and a properly formatted title block. (7 Marks)"**

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Analyze the component (1 mark):**
The bushing has rotational symmetry about its main axis and contains an internal bore with a key slot. The component is **axially symmetric**.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply view count formula (1 mark):**
$$
\text{View Configuration} = \{ \text{Front View (full longitudinal section)}, \text{End View (circular with key slot)} \}
$$

A **full longitudinal section** of the front view is chosen because the internal bore and key slot would otherwise require excessive hidden lines, violating the clarity rule ($N_{\text{hidden}} > 4$).

**Step 3 ‚Äî CAD setup (2 marks):**
- Sheet: **A3 (420 √ó 297 mm)** with border 10 mm from edges.
- Layers created: `Construction`, `Centerline`, `Object`, `Hidden`, `Hatch`, `Dimension`, `Text`, `Border`.
- Grid: 5 mm snap; units: **millimeters**.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Draw the views (2 marks):**
- Front view drawn as **full section** with cutting plane A-A passing through the central axis.
- Centerlines drawn (chain thin) along the axis and through the key slot.
- Hatching applied at **45¬∞** with **2.5 mm spacing** on the cut cylindrical wall.
- End view drawn to the **right** of the front view (First Angle convention).
- Hidden lines omitted in the sectioned front view (they are replaced by hatching).

**Step 5 ‚Äî Projection symbol (1 mark):**
First angle projection symbol (truncated cone, small end to the right) inserted in the title block.

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Dimensioning strategy (2 marks):**
- All **lengths** (outer lengths of each step) ‚Äî parallel-dimensioned from the left end-face datum.
- All **diameters** ‚Äî using **√ò symbol**, placed on the longitudinal (front) view.
- **Key slot width** and **depth** ‚Äî directly dimensioned on the sectioned front view.
- **Concentricity** of the bore with the outer surface ‚Äî implied by common centerline (no separate dimension needed).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Tolerances (2 marks):**
- Outer diameter √ò 60: **¬± 0.05 mm** (general running fit).
- Inner bore √ò 40: **H7 tolerance** ($^{+0.000}_{+0.025}$ mm) ‚Äî for bearing press fit.
- Key slot width: **N9 tolerance** ($^{+0.000}_{-0.036}$ mm).
- Geometric tolerance: **concentricity** √ò 0.02 mm between outer and inner diameters with respect to datum A (left end face).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Surface finish (1 mark):**
- Internal bore surface: **Ra 0.8 ¬µm** (fine finish for bearing seat).
- Outer cylindrical surfaces: **Ra 1.6 ¬µm**.
- End faces: **Ra 3.2 ¬µm**.
- Unspecified surfaces: **Ra 6.3 ¬µm** (note in title block).

**Step 4 ‚Äî Title block (2 marks):**
A standard KTU-format title block inserted at the **bottom-right** containing:
- Part Name: **BUSHING**
- DWG No: **BSH-2024-005**
- Material: **EN8 Steel (IS 1570)**
- Scale: **1:1**
- Sheet: **1 of 1**
- Drawn By / Date, Checked By / Date
- Projection Symbol: **First Angle**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice:**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Explain the procedure for creating a part drawing of a given machine component in a CAD environment. Your answer must cover:**
**(a) The step-by-step workflow from view selection to final plotting, with justification for each step. (7 Marks)**
**(b) The drafting standards (IS codes) governing line types, dimensioning, sectional views, and title block layout. (7 Marks)"**

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Component Analysis & View Selection (1.5 marks):**
Identify symmetry, internal features, and complexity. Apply the view count decision formula:
$$
\text{Views} = f(\text{Symmetry}, \text{Internal Features}, \text{Complexity})
$$
Choose front view as the most informative face, then project top and side views.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Sheet & Layer Setup (1 mark):**
Select A3 or A4 sheet per IS 10711. Create CAD layers: `Construction`, `Object`, `Hidden`, `Centerline`, `Hatch`, `Dimension`, `Text`, `Border`.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Construction & Projection (1.5 marks):**
Draw light construction lines to position views. Use CAD projection tools to project from front to top and side, ensuring alignment (vertical alignment between front and top, horizontal alignment between front and side).

**Step 4 ‚Äî Object Line Conversion (1 mark):**
Convert construction geometry to thick continuous object lines (0.5 mm). Add hidden lines (thin dashed) for non-visible features. Insert centerlines (chain thin).

**Step 5 ‚Äî Sectioning, Dimensioning & Detailing (1.5 marks):**
Apply sectional view if required (cutting plane line + hatching at 45¬∞). Dimension all features per the "dimension once" rule. Add tolerances, surface finish symbols, and notes.

**Step 6 ‚Äî Title Block & Plotting (0.5 marks):**
Insert KTU-format title block with all metadata. Verify the drawing against the original component. Plot at correct scale (typically 1:1 for small parts).

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**IS 696:1972 ‚Äî Line Types (2 marks):**
- **Thick continuous** ‚Äî visible edges, object lines (0.5 mm).
- **Thin continuous** ‚Äî dimension lines, extension lines, leader lines, hatching (0.25 mm).
- **Thin dashed** ‚Äî hidden edges (~3 mm dashes, 1 mm gaps).
- **Chain thin** ‚Äî centerlines (long‚Äìshort‚Äìlong pattern).
- **Chain thick** ‚Äî cutting plane lines and section indications.

**IS 15021 ‚Äî Dimensioning Rules (2 marks):**
- Dimensions placed **between views**, not inside them.
- **10 mm spacing** between parallel dimension lines.
- Arrowheads: **3 mm long, 1 mm wide** filled triangles.
- **Dimension once rule:** no feature dimensioned twice.
- √ò symbol for diameters, R for radii.

**IS 696 ‚Äî Sectional Views (1.5 marks):**
- Hatching at **45¬∞** to horizontal, spacing 2‚Äì3 mm.
- Same part ‚Üí same hatch; different parts ‚Üí different hatch directions.
- Cutting plane indicated by chain thick line with arrows showing viewing direction.
- Section labels (A-A) above both the section view and the cutting plane location.

**IS 15021 ‚Äî Title Block (1.5 marks):**
- Located at the **bottom-right** of the drawing sheet.
- Contains: company name/logo, drawing title, drawing number, scale, sheet number, material, drawn by, checked by, approved by, projection symbol, sheet size, revision number.

---

## 5.3 ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong projection method (‚Äì2 to ‚Äì3 marks):** Drawing the **Third Angle** projection when First Angle is required. The **top view below the front view** is the dead giveaway.
>
> 2. **Missing or incorrect title block (‚Äì2 marks):** Forgetting the **projection symbol**, leaving fields blank, or placing the title block in the wrong corner.
>
> 3. **Dual dimensioning (‚Äì1 to ‚Äì2 marks):** Dimensioning the same feature in two different views ‚Äî violates the "dimension once" rule.
>
> 4. **Wrong line types (‚Äì1 mark each):** Using thick lines for hidden edges or thin lines for visible edges. This is a **fatal drafting error** in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Incorrect hatching (‚Äì1 to ‚Äì2 marks):** Hatching at the wrong angle (not 45¬∞), wrong spacing, or hatching solid parts that should not be sectioned (e.g., shafts, bolts, nuts, rivets, keys ‚Äî these are NOT sectioned even if the cutting plane passes through them).
>
> 6. **Missing centerlines (‚Äì1 mark):** Every circle, hole, and cylindrical feature must have a centerline passing through its center.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the √ò symbol (‚Äì1 mark per occurrence):** Diameters without the √ò symbol are treated as linear dimensions ‚Äî a **critical functional error** since √ò30 ‚âÝ 30 mm length.
>
> 8. **Dimensioning inside the view (‚Äì1 mark):** All dimensions should be placed **outside** the view, between the views, on extension lines.

---

## 5.4 üîë Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision ‚Äî The 15 Golden Points:**
>
> 1. ‚úÖ **First Angle Projection** is the KTU standard ‚Äî top view **below** front, left side view on the **right**.
> 2. ‚úÖ **Object lines** are **thick continuous (0.5 mm)**; **hidden lines** are **thin dashed**.
> 3. ‚úÖ **Centerlines** are **chain thin** with a **long‚Äìshort‚Äìlong** pattern.
> 4. ‚úÖ **Dimension every feature exactly once** ‚Äî never dimension the same feature in two views.
> 5. ‚úÖ Always use the **√ò symbol** for diameters and **R** for radii.
> 6. ‚úÖ Hatching is always at **45¬∞** with **2‚Äì3 mm spacing**.
> 7. ‚úÖ **Shafts, bolts, nuts, rivets, keys, pins, and balls are NOT sectioned** even if the cutting plane passes through them.
> 8. ‚úÖ Place dimensions **between views**, not inside them.
> 9. ‚úÖ Maintain a **10 mm gap** between the object and the nearest dimension line.
> 10. ‚úÖ The **title block** goes in the **bottom-right** corner with the projection symbol visible.
> 11. ‚úÖ Arrowheads are **3 mm long, 1 mm wide** filled triangles.
> 12. ‚úÖ Lettering is **5 mm uppercase** for notes, **7 mm** for titles (IS 9601).
> 13. ‚úÖ Hidden lines can be **omitted** in a sectioned view (replaced by hatching).
> 14. ‚úÖ **Datum selection** should be a physically accessible face/edge for the machinist.
> 15. ‚úÖ Always **plot at 1:1 scale** for small parts; use standard reduction scales (1:2, 1:5, 1:10) for larger components.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Assembly Drawing** is a **technical orthographic projection** that depicts the spatial relationship, mating surfaces, relative position, and functional fit of two or more machine components when they are joined together to form a complete sub-assembly, mechanism, or machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (IS 696 Compliant):** An assembly drawing is a drawing that shows the various parts of a machine or structure in their **relative assembled positions**, including the necessary sectional views, hidden details, centre lines, and a **Bill of Materials (BOM)** indicating part numbers, names, quantities, and materials.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Assembly Drawings (as per IS 696 & KTU Lab Manual)

| **Type** | **Definition** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **General Assembly Drawing** | Shows all parts in their working positions | Workshop, KTU Lab Exam |
| **Installation Drawing** | Shows the assembly in its foundation/setting | Site installation |
| **Sub-Assembly Drawing** | Shows a group of parts forming a sub-unit | Modular manufacturing |
| **Working Drawing / Detail Drawing** | Shows **one single component** with full dimensions | Workshop machining |
| **Schematic / Diagram Assembly** | Shows only the operating principle (lines & symbols) | Patent/Manual design |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äì "IKEA Instruction Manual"**
> Imagine you bought a flat-pack wardrobe from IKEA. The manual shows you **all the wooden panels, screws, dowels, hinges, and rails** in **one single exploded picture** with numbered arrows, so you understand *where each piece goes* and *how they fit together* to form the final wardrobe. The **master parts list** at the back is the **Bill of Materials (BOM)**.
>
> An **Assembly Drawing does exactly the same thing for a machine**: a **cotter joint, knuckle joint, or screw jack** is shown as a *single, fully dimensioned, sectioned drawing* that a fitter in a workshop can use to *assemble* the parts without ambiguity.

### 1.2.1 Why is Assembly Drawing so important in Mechanical Engineering?

1. **Manufacturing Communication** ‚Äì It is the **"language of the shop floor"** between the design office and the fitter.
2. **Tolerance Stack-up Verification** ‚Äì Shows the *mating clearances and interferences* between mating parts.
3. **Maintenance & Servicing** ‚Äì Mechanics use assembly drawings to *disassemble, repair, and reassemble* machinery.
4. **Interchangeability** ‚Äì Ensures that *mass-produced parts* can be replaced without re-machining.
5. **KTU 2024 CAD Lab Skill** ‚Äì This experiment is the **bridge between the 2D projection (Module Exp 1) and 3D Modelling (Module Exp 4/5)**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Drawing Conventions (KTU 2024 Mandatory Compliance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) ‚Äì IS 696 (1989) & IS 15021 (2001)** govern all Indian engineering drawing practice. KTU 2024 Scheme requires strict adherence.

| **Convention** | **Rule** |
|---|---|
| **Sectioning adjacent parts** | Hatching lines must be drawn at **45¬∞**, in **opposite directions** for adjacent (touching) parts. |
| **Same part sectioning** | Hatching direction & spacing must be **identical** for the same part appearing in different sections. |
| **Hidden lines** | Generally **omitted** in assembly drawings (use full-section / half-section instead). |
| **Centre lines** | Must pass through the **axis of symmetry** of all circular components (holes, shafts, pins). |
| **Dimensional priority** | Functional dimensions > Assembly dimensions > Overall (reference) dimensions. |
| **Leader lines** | Used for **part numbers** in balloon format; they must end with a **dot or arrowhead** *on the part*, never crossing another line. |
| **Bill of Materials (BOM)** | Placed in the **top-right corner** of the drawing sheet, above the title block. |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow ‚Äì "How to Make an Assembly Drawing"

The KTU 2024 CAD Lab examiners follow a strict **8-step workflow** when evaluating assembly drawings. Understanding this is *crucial* for scoring full marks.

### Step-by-Step Logic (Bulleted Flow)

1. **Study the parts list & pictorial view** ‚Äì Identify the *function* of each part.
2. **Decide the principal viewing plane** ‚Äì The position showing the *maximum number of mating features* is the **front view**.
3. **Select a sectioning strategy**:
   - **Full Section**: if internal details are complex.
   - **Half Section**: if symmetry exists (used for most joints).
   - **Offset Section**: to show non-collinear features in a single plane.
4. **Draw the centre lines** ‚Äì All axes of symmetry, including bolt circles, pin axes, and shaft centres.
5. **Block the major parts first** ‚Äì Draw the *base / socket / body* in light construction lines.
6. **Add the mating parts** ‚Äì Insert pins, keys, cotters, nuts, bolts, washers in correct sequence.
7. **Apply sectioning & hatching** ‚Äì Hatch *adjacent parts* in **opposite directions** at **45¬∞**.
8. **Dimension, annotate, and create BOM** ‚Äì Mark overall dimensions, leader-line part numbers, balloon the part list.

---

## 2.2 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **standard proportional formulas** used in Khurmi & Gupta (most cited KTU reference) and the KTU CAD Lab manual. The letter **$d$** always refers to the *nominal shaft diameter* or *nominal pin diameter* in **millimetres (mm)**, and *stress values* in **N/mm¬≤**.

### 2.2.1 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Cotter Joint (Tensile/Compression Joint)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Width of cotter (at the back) $b_1$ | $b_1 = 0.45 \, d$ | Tapered from $b_1$ to $b$ at the open end |
| Width of cotter (at the open end) $b$ | $b = 0.4 \, d$ | Allowable range: $0.3 d$ to $0.5 d$ |
| Thickness of cotter $t$ | $t = 0.31 \, d$ | Range: $0.30 d$ to $0.35 d$ |
| Taper of cotter | $1 : 10$ to $1 : 20$ | $1:20$ preferred for steel; $1:10$ for brittle cast iron |
| Length of cotter $L_c$ | $L_c = 2b + 12 \text{ mm (approx.)}$ | For solid socket-spigot joint |
| Outer diameter of socket $D_2$ | $D_2 = 1.5 d + 13 \text{ mm}$ | Socket collar outer dia |
| Inner diameter of spigot | $d_1 = 1.5 d + 13 \text{ mm}$ | Solid spigot cross-section (bigger) |
| Shear stress in cotter $\tau_c$ | $\tau_c = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot t}$ | $P$ = axial tensile load (N) |
| Crushing / Bearing stress $\sigma_b$ | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot t_1}$ | $t_1$ = depth of socket collar |
| Width of slot $b$ | $b = 0.45 d$ to $0.5 d$ | Spigot & socket slot width = cotter width |

### 2.2.2 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Knuckle Joint (Tension Joint, Angular Movement)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Diameter of knuckle pin $d$ | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\pi \tau}}$ | $P$ = tensile load, $\tau$ = permissible shear stress (‚âà 40 N/mm¬≤) |
| Outer diameter of eye $D_1$ | $D_1 = 2.2 d$ | Standard proportion |
| Outer diameter of fork end $D$ | $D = 3 d$ | Standard proportion |
| Inner diameter of eye (one end) $d_1$ | $d_1 = d$ | Hole through the eye |
| Inner diameter of fork end (other end) $d_2$ | $d_2 = 1.1 d$ | Wider hole for the pin head/collar |
| Width of eye / fork $b$ | $b = 1.5 d$ | Range: $1.4 d$ to $1.6 d$ |
| Width of fork end (single side) $b_1$ | $b_1 = 0.75 b$ | So two sides form full width $b$ |
| Length of pin $L_p$ | $L_p = 2 b_1 + b$ | One side of fork + eye + other side of fork |
| Shear failure load of pin | $P_{shear} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ | Double shear in knuckle joint |
| Crushing failure load | $P_{crush} = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | $t$ = thickness of eye/fork |

### 2.2.3 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Flanged Coupling (Rigid Shaft Coupling)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Flange diameter $D$ | $D = 3.5 d + 15 \text{ mm}$ | Range: $3 d$ to $4 d$ |
| Pitch circle diameter $D_1$ | $D_1 = 3 d + 15 \text{ mm}$ | Centre of bolt circle |
| Flange thickness $t_f$ | $t_f = 0.5 d$ | Unwin's formula: $t_f = \sqrt{d}$ |
| Number of bolts $n$ | $n = \dfrac{3 d}{5}$ | Rounded to nearest integer (4, 6, 8) |
| Bolt diameter $d_b$ | $d_b = 0.5 d$ to $0.625 d$ | Standard sizes: M10, M12, M16 |
| Hub diameter $D_2$ | $D_2 = 1.6 d + 13 \text{ mm}$ | Shaft collar hub |
| Hub length $L_h$ | $L_h = 1.5 d$ | Range: $1.25 d$ to $1.5 d$ |
| Shear stress in bolt | $\tau_b = \dfrac{2T}{n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_b^2 \cdot D_1}$ | $T$ = torque transmitted (N¬∑mm) |
| Crushing stress on flange | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{2T}{n \cdot d_b \cdot t_f \cdot D_1}$ | Bearing on flange face |

### 2.2.4 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Screw Jack (Lifting Machine)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Mean screw diameter $d_m$ | $d_m = d_o - 0.5 p$ | $d_o$ = outer dia, $p$ = pitch |
| Lead $L$ | $L = p$ (single start) | Or $L = n \cdot p$ ($n$ = no. of starts) |
| Helix angle $\alpha$ | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_m}$ | Critical for self-locking check |
| Friction angle $\phi$ | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | $\mu$ ‚âà 0.1 to 0.15 for lubricated steel |
| Effort required (lifting) $P$ | $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | $W$ = load lifted (N) |
| Effort required (lowering) $P_1$ | $P_1 = W \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | For self-locking screws ($\alpha < \phi$) |
| Mechanical Advantage (MA) | $\text{MA} = \dfrac{2 \pi l}{p}$ | $l$ = length of lever arm (mm) |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} \times 100\,\%$ | Lifting efficiency |
| Maximum efficiency condition | $\alpha = 45¬∞ - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | Independent of load (only friction) |
| Self-locking condition | $\alpha \le \phi$ | If helix angle ‚â§ friction angle |

### 2.2.5 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Universal / Hooke's Joint (Misaligned Shaft)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Torque $T$ | $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau \, d^3$ | $d$ = shaft diameter, $\tau$ = allowable shear |
| Shaft diameter $d$ | $d = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 T}{\pi \tau}}$ | From torsion equation |
| Velocity ratio of shafts (Hooke's) | $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos \alpha}{1 - \sin^2 \alpha \cdot \cos^2 \theta_1}$ | $\alpha$ = angle of inclination, $\theta_1$ = driver angle |
| Condition for uniform velocity | $\alpha = 0¬∞$ (or 180¬∞) | Collinear shafts only |

### 2.2.6 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Plummer Block (Bearing Housing)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Bearing length $L_b$ | $L_b = 1.5 d$ to $2 d$ | $d$ = journal diameter |
| Cap thickness $t_c$ | $t_c = 0.08 d + 6 \text{ mm}$ | Top cap of block |
| Base thickness $t_b$ | $t_b = 0.12 d + 8 \text{ mm}$ | Bottom base of block |
| Bolt size (cap) | M12 to M20 | Two cap bolts symmetrically placed |
| Foot bolt size | M16 to M24 | Two foundation bolts |

### 2.2.7 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Oldham's Coupling (Parallel Misalignment)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter $d$ | $d = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 T}{\pi \tau}}$ | Same torsion formula |
| Hub diameter $D_h$ | $D_h = 2 d$ to $2.5 d$ | Square or round hub |
| Flange diameter $D$ | $D = 3 d + 15 \text{ mm}$ | Similar to flanged coupling |
| Maximum misalignment $e$ | $e \le 0.25 d$ | Sideways offset between shafts |

### 2.2.8 Master Formula Table ‚Äì Friction Clutch (Multi-Plate Cone Clutch)

| **Component / Dimension** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Axial thrust (cone clutch) $W$ | $W = \dfrac{2 T \sin \alpha}{\mu (D_o + D_i)}$ | $\alpha$ = semi-cone angle |
| Uniform pressure theory (friction) $T$ | $T = \dfrac{\pi \mu W (R_o^2 - R_i^2)(R_o + R_i)}{3 \sin \alpha}$ | Plate clutch with $n$ pairs: $T_n = n \cdot T_1$ |
